title: Managing Application Secrets
description:
## Where Should the DB Password Live?

Your application needs a **database password** at runtime. The code lives in a Git repository and is built into a container image by CI. What is the **best** way to handle this secret?

options.text: Store it in a dedicated **secrets manager** (HashiCorp Vault, a cloud secret manager, or Sealed Secrets) that provides encryption, access control, rotation and auditing, and inject it into the app at runtime
Commit the password in plain text to the Git repository so every developer can find it easily
Hardcode it into the Dockerfile / bake it into the container image during the build
Paste it into a comment in the README so it's documented

explanation: Secrets should **never** be committed to Git or baked into images — both are widely distributed, often cached, and trivially leaked (anyone with the repo or image can extract them, and Git history keeps them forever even after a 'fix'). The correct approach is a **secrets manager** such as HashiCorp Vault, a cloud provider's secret manager, or Sealed Secrets, which keeps the value **encrypted**, enforces **access control**, supports **rotation**, and provides an **audit** trail. The app fetches or has the secret **injected at runtime** (e.g. as an env var or mounted file), so the secret never lives in source control or the image. Options (b), (c) and (d) all expose the secret.

hints.title: Anything in Git or the image is public-ish
hints.body: Repos get cloned and images get pulled by many people and systems. Treat both as places where a committed secret is effectively compromised — keep secrets in a manager and inject them at runtime.
